failure_scenarios.description: A non-obvious failure: the Tesla/LG battery megaplant and broader US domestic manufacturing buildout absorbs available high-yield credit capacity, crowding out existing HYG constituents from refinancing markets and triggering a 'good economy, bad credit' spread widening as investment-grade capex competes with junk-rated rollover demand — HYG loses 10-13% over 3-4 months.
failure_scenarios.reasoning: The IC highlights a $4.3B Tesla/LG battery plant and broader domestic energy storage manufacturing acceleration. In a LATE_EXPANSION regime with persistently elevated rates, an infrastructure investment supercycle creates a paradox: strong nominal growth supports the 'no recession' narrative (suppressing risk-off HY demand) but simultaneously tightens credit availability for lower-rated borrowers as IG issuers crowd the primary market. This breaks the historical correlation where 'strong economy
ic_evidence_for: zerohedge: Tesla/LG $4.3B LFP battery plant in Michigan signals major domestic manufacturing capex acceleration — IG issuers will dominate primary credit markets, crowding HY rollover.
zerohedge: Global billionaire wealth expansion driven by AI and tech entrepreneurship — capital allocation shifting toward equity/IG and away from HY as a yield vehicle.
forward_guidance: Agricultural supply shock and energy shock together create a bifurcated economy where capital-intensive sectors boom while consumer-facing HY issuers face margin squeeze.
ic_evidence_against: No IC coverage: No analyst in the provided IC sources directly addresses the IG/HY primary market crowding mechanism.
THR_ENERGY_20260317_001: Oil market efficiency thesis (conviction 8.4) suggests credit markets are also efficiently allocating — crowding effects would be priced incrementally, not as a sudden shock.
zerohedge: US economic primacy narrative broadly supports continued credit market depth and capacity — a $4.3B plant is unlikely to materially tighten conditions for the entire HY universe.
failure_scenarios.early_warning_indicator: IG primary market issuance exceeds $80B in a single month while HY new issue volume drops below $15B in the same month, concurrent with HYG OAS widening >30bps despite investment-grade spreads tightening — confirming the bifurcated credit market dynamic.
failure_scenarios.early_warning_source: SIFMA monthly bond issuance statistics + Bloomberg IG vs HY new issue league tables + FRED BAMLH0A0HYM2 vs BAMLC0A0CM spread differential
failure_scenarios.portfolio_impact: -10 to -13% on HYG position; portfolio-level impact -2.9% to -3.7% given 28.8% weight

A ceasefire announcement — even partial — would collapse the war premium simultaneously across DBC's energy-heavy basket (~35-40% WTI/energy exposure). failure_scenarios.description: Global demand destruction from Europe and Asia recession — triggered by Hormuz-induced energy shock — overwhelms supply disruption and causes commodity complex collapse across DBC's entire basket, with DBC losing 18-25%.
failure_scenarios.reasoning: forward_guidance explicitly warns the Hormuz closure creates an asymmetric shock where Europe and Asia enter recession while the US remains insulated. In a LATE_EXPANSION regime, this is the classic demand-side unwind: commodity prices initially spike on supply shock, then crater 6-10 weeks later as destruction of industrial demand (European manufacturing, Asian import-driven economies) overwhelms the supply reduction. failure_scenarios.description: Crowded commodity-inflation positioning unwinds simultaneously across HYG and DBC as LATE_EXPANSION regime tips to contraction, triggering correlated liquidation that overwhelms DBC's diversification benefit and amplifies losses to 12-18%.
failure_scenarios.reasoning: The V16 portfolio holds 49.1% in two overtly inflation/risk-on assets (HYG 28.8% + DBC 20.3%) that are historically correlated in late-cycle risk-off episodes. In a LATE_EXPANSION regime inflecting toward contraction, institutional commodity funds that entered on the Hormuz supply shock narrative would face simultaneous redemption pressure — a repeat of the Q4 2018 or March 2020 pattern where commodities and credit sold off together despite different fundamental drivers.
